g.data-narration: Welcome to Java Loops! This lecture covers all loop types from basic for loops to advanced nested structures and control statements.
g.text: A Complete Guide to Iteration Control Structures

g.data-narration: Loops automate repetitive tasks, process arrays, and enable efficient algorithms. They are essential for any Java programmer.
Java provides four main loop types: for loops for known iterations, for-each for collections, while for unknown iterations, and do-while for guaranteed execution.
g.text: Four Types of Loops in Java

g.data-narration: A for loop has three parts: initialization sets the starting value, condition checks before each iteration, and increment updates the counter.
g.text: Flow: Init - Check Condition - Execute Body - Increment - Repeat

g.data-narration: Here is a practical for loop example printing numbers 1 through 5. The loop variable i starts at 1 and increments until reaching 5.
The enhanced for-each loop simplifies iterating through arrays and collections without index management. Use colon syntax to iterate.
g.text: Cleaner code but cannot modify elements, no index access, forward only

g.data-narration: The while loop executes repeatedly while a condition remains true. Perfect when iteration count is unknown before starting.
The do-while loop guarantees at least one execution since the condition is checked after the loop body runs.
Nested loops place one loop inside another. The outer loop controls rows while the inner loop controls columns. Used for patterns and 2D arrays.
The break statement immediately terminates the loop and transfers control to the statement after the loop. Useful for early exit conditions.
The continue statement skips the current iteration and jumps to the next one. Perfect for filtering unwanted values without exiting the loop.
Labels allow break and continue to control outer loops from within nested loops. Use labelName colon before a loop, then break labelName to exit it.
Two essential loop patterns: calculating sum by accumulating values, and finding maximum by comparing each element to the current max.
g.text: int sum = 0; for (int n : numbers) sum += n;
int max = arr[0]; for (int i = 1; i < n; i++) if (arr[i] > max) max = arr[i];
Initialize accumulator, loop, add each element
Set first as max, compare each, update if larger

g.data-narration: Array reversal swaps elements from opposite ends moving toward the center. Counting uses a counter variable incremented on each match.
g.text: for (i=0; i<len/2; i++) { temp=arr[i]; arr[i]=arr[len-1-i]; arr[len-1-i]=temp; }
int count = 0; for (int i = 0; i < str.length(); i++) if (str.charAt(i) == target) count++;
